perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees it shall apply the same level of confidential treatment to the other party's Confidential Information as it does to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to try to apply a similar level of confidential treatment to the other party's Confidential Information as it applies to its own Confidential Information, but no guarantees are made.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'try to apply a similar level' and 'but no guarantees are made' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This could lead to inconsistent application of security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 352-C prohibits fraudulent practices in securities transactions. Weakening the confidentiality obligation surrounding sensitive business information involved in a potential strategic alliance could be construed as facilitating such practices, especially if the information is later misused for insider trading or market manipulation. The original agreement sought to prevent such leaks that may cause violations to this clause.

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GBS - General Business Article 23-A - Fraudulent Practices in Respect to Stocks, Bonds and Other Securities 352-C - Prohibited Acts Constituting Misdemeanor; Felony. Universal Citation: NY Gen Bus L § 352-C (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 352-c. Prohibited acts constituting misdemeanor; felony. 1. It shall be illegal and prohibited for any person, partnership, corporation, company, trust or association, or any agent or employee thereof, to use or employ any of the following acts or practices: (a) Any fraud, deception, concealment, suppression, false pretense or fictitious or pretended purchase or sale; (b) Any promise or representation as to the future which is beyond reasonable expectation or unwarranted by existing circumstances; (c) Any representation or statement which is false, where the person who made such representation or statement: (i) knew the truth; or (ii) with reasonable effort could have known the truth; or (iii) made no reasonable effort to ascertain the truth; or (iv) did not have knowledge concerning the representation or statement made; where engaged in to induce or promote the issuance, distribution, exchange, sale, negotiation or purchase within or from this state of any securities or commodities, as defined in section three hundred fifty-two of this article, regardless of whether issuance, distribution, exchange, sale, negotiation or purchase resulted. 2. It shall be illegal and prohibited for any person, partnership, corporation, company, trust or association, or any agent or employee thereof, to eng
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligation by adding 'try to apply' and 'but no guarantees are made,' creating ambiguity. The original text mandated a specific level of confidential treatment, whereas the changed text introduces uncertainty. New York General Business Law § 352-C prohibits fraudulent practices in securities transactions. By weakening the confidentiality obligation surrounding sensitive business information, the change introduces ambiguity. This could potentially facilitate practices that could lead to violations under § 352-C, particularly if the information is later misused, thus contradicting the intent to prevent fraudulent activity related to securities as described.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby acknowledge that disclosure of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof would cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party. Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available, each party is entitled to equitable relief through injunction if the other party or any of its employees, agents or representatives breaches any provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties suspect that disclosure of Confidential Information in violation of the terms might cause some harm. Each party may consider seeking equitable relief if there seems to be a significant breach.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'would cause irreparable harm' to 'might cause some harm' weakens the acknowledgment of potential harm. Additionally, replacing 'is entitled to equitable relief' with 'may consider seeking equitable relief' makes seeking an injunction discretionary rather than a right, undermining the enforceability of this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement isn't directly a sale of goods contract, NY UCC § 2-716 addresses specific performance which is akin to the equitable relief through injunction mentioned in the contract. By casting doubt on the potential for 'irreparable harm', the modification makes it harder to argue for specific performance or an injunction in case of a breach, as a party must typically demonstrate that monetary damages are inadequate, and that the harm is irreparable.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-716 Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 7 Section 2--716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2) The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. In the case of goods bought for personal, family or household purposes, the buyer's right of replevin vests upon acquisition of a special property, even if the seller had not then repudiated or failed to deliver.
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges 'irreparable harm' and 'entitlement to equitable relief,' whereas the changed text weakens this to 'might cause some harm' and 'may consider seeking equitable relief.' The LLM explanation points to NY UCC § 2-716 regarding specific performance, akin to equitable relief (injunction). By casting doubt on 'irreparable harm,' the modified contract makes it more difficult to argue for specific performance or an injunction. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing NY UCC § 2-716, states that 'Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances.' By weakening the acknowledgment of harm and making equitable relief discretionary, the changed text contradicts the potential for specific performance/equitable relief as outlined in the law snippet, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the enforceability of a clause intended to protect confidential information.
